GENERAL INSPECTION:
(Perform a walk-through of your property the way a home inspector would, looking at the “big picture.”)
Are there any cracks in your walls that imply the foundation may be shifting?
Is your roof in good condition? When was it last replaced? (Most roofs need to be replaced every 20 years.)
Are there any signs of water damage in your attic, basement, laundry room, kitchen, or bathrooms?
Does your plumbing make any unusual noises when you turn it on? (We recommend getting your sewer line
cleaned once per year.)
Are there any overarching issues with your electricity, such as frequent outages?
Are there any large trees nearby with roots that encroach on your foundation, or branches that threaten to scrape
your roof or damage the property?
Are there any signs of pest damage?
VACATION NECESSITIES:
1) Replace all the batteries in your smoke and CO detectors every 6 months – whether they need it or not! Have
extra batteries on hand.
2) Replace light bulbs with LED bulbs (can last up to 5 years); have extra bulbs on hand
3) Check that the following safety items are available and in good working order:
a) Fire extinguishers
b) First aid kits
c) Smoke/CO detectors
4) Be sure your audio/visual systems and electronic devices are working.
Televisions
DVD/Blu-ray players
Stereos and speakers
Video game consoles
Internet router and modem
INTERIOR MAINTENANCE (each room)
Downloaded on Tuesday, Mar 10, 2026 at 02:08 AM
Page 4 of 23
Floors – Wood floors may need to be refinished or treated, carpets need to be shampooed and cleaned (and
sometimes replaced), and stone floors may need to be resealed.
Windows – Replace any cracked or damaged windows
Doorknobs & Cabinet Handles – Check and tighten all the screws to avoid rattly handles that can eventually work
their way loose
Furniture – Leather furniture may need to be conditioned, fabric furniture may need repairs or cleaning, and wood
furniture may need to be refinished or treated.
Plumbing Fixtures – Double-check your water pressure and drainage for any problems, and replace any fixtures
that are looking worn or loose; check temperature of water heater
Replace batteries in the remotes.
Re-balance or replace wobbly, noisy ceiling fans.
If you have blinds, check to make sure they still work properly and the cords / controls aren't tangled or missing.
EXTERIOR MAINTENANCE
The first thing you’ll want to do every year is assess whether your house needs to be repainted. “Curb appeal” is
one of the first things your renters will see when they arrive, and you want to be sure they have the best possible
impression of your house.
We recommend repainting every five years or so, but take the time during your six-month inspection to see if paint
needs to be touched up anywhere.
You should also look at your landscaping for any improvements or changes that need to be made – your guests
will definitely notice if your front or back yards aren’t looking pristine.
Beyond aesthetics, you should also:
Clean your gutters and downspout and check that drainage is working as it should
Check your pathways for any damage that might make them difficult or dangerous to navigate
Assess your patio furniture for necessary repairs or maintenance (wood patio furniture may need resealing, for
example)
Check your sprinklers to be sure they’re working properly and watering your lawn, rather than the sidewalk
Trim your trees away from the home to be sure they’re compliant with fire standards in your area
Aerate your lawn if necessary
MAJOR MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
Every six months, change the filters in your major mechanical systems and do a visual inspection for any
problems.
We highly recommend scheduling your six-month check just before a new season begins so that you can be sure
the mechanical systems you’ll need are in good working order. For example, you’ll be able to check that the
furnace is working properly right before it’s time to turn on the heat for fall or winter.
Be sure to change the filters in your:
Furnace
Air conditioner
Dryer
Ventilation system
One place to double-check is the bathroom! The exhaust vents in bathrooms often get far more clogged than
other places in the house, so be sure you specifically take the cover off and take a look around in the vent pipe. If
it looks pretty clogged in there, clean it thoroughly before replacing the cover.
Duct system
CLEANING
Semi-annual deep clean, usually performed during your off season. A deep clean includes:
Washing curtains
Flipping mattresses
Washing mattress covers
Deep-cleaning rugs
Treating wood surfaces
Light repairs to paint
Steam-cleaning or conditioning furniture

Deep-cleaning the oven
Windows Inside and Out
Straighten outside furniture - bring in or put out seat cushions
Clean grill area; stock mini frig with soft drinks

09-28-16 I spoke to Greg Mangum and he said the roof looked fine except for leak behind chimney. He noticed it
when he was in attic. I questioned the valuation on the roof permit from 2007 and he said that permitting in FL is
not an absolute science...that valuations are all over the board. I also told him what contractors were telling
Randy...that they would not put their name on repairs when a roof needed to be replaced. Greg's comment was
"Well of course they want to sell you a whole roof...these big companies don't want to waste their time on repairs."
Nobody is honest anymore. So he had recommended Henricks Roofing to LZ and they are coming 9-30-16. He
also said that Lester Paulk and Son were good and did roof repairs. Greg said he didn't really look at the flat roof
on the balcony but noticed the tiles/tarp laying there. He said those flat roofs can last 18-20 years if they are
installed correctly and waterproof sealer used. Because of the sun though, they need to be maintained and
inspected periodically if you want them to last a long time. If the balcony is going to be fixed, it will need to be
permitted. No contractor will agree to laying a membrane over existing tile and then new tile on top of that. It would
not pass inspection. Also, you want to get a reputable company to tackle that job because if it's not done correctly,
it will open up a whole new can of worms and structural issues will arise if membrane/materials aren't right. Also,
RS was told that the doors would need to be replaced because code requires that the membrane come up and
over the threshold... so the door tracks would have to be removed and they would be next to impossible to
reinstall. Code has changed for those doors anyway. It would be like installing a brand new sink and then
replacing the rusty old faucet in it. DS
